one_line_takeaway: SaLAD shows that on benign, real-world daily-life queries with hidden visual hazards, even top MLLMs give an adequate safety warning only ~57% of the time (avg 30.65% across 18 models), and existing jailbreak-style safety alignment (VLGuard, MIS, SPA-VL) barely helps or actively hurts -- the bottleneck is applying known safety knowledge via cross-modal reasoning, not lacking the knowledge itself.
key_finding: Across 18 evaluated MLLMs/MLRMs, the best model (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) reaches only 57.20% Accuracy Rate on the unsafe subset (overall 77.05%, safe 99.58%), and the average unsafe-subset accuracy across all 18 models is 30.65% -- far below the near-ceiling performance most models show on the safe subset, indicating systematic failure to proactively flag real-world hazards despite low false-refusal rates. Closed-source models (GPT-4o 53.83% unsafe/75.36% overall, Gemini 2.5 Flash 55.05%/75.96%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 57.20%/77.05%) clearly outperform open-source models, whose unsafe-subset scores range from 10.93% (Deepseek-VL2-Tiny) to 37.10% (LLaVA-OneVision). Retrofitted safety-alignment methods give only marginal or inconsistent gains and sometimes hurt: on Llama-3.2-Vision-11B, VLGuard drops overall accuracy from 63.00% to 42.00% (safe accuracy collapses from 94.00% to 54.50%) while only partially raising unsafe accuracy (32.00% to 29.50%, actually a slight decrease), whereas SPA-VL is the only method giving consistent gains across all three tested backbones (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B unsafe accuracy rises only ~1%, from 33.00% to 35.00%). Image captions do not reliably help (unlike prior findings in VLSBench/ECSO): captions provide no benefit for InternVL2.5-8B-MPO and Qwen2.5-VL-7B and only a ~4.75% gain for Llama-3.2-Vision-11B, suggesting SaLAD's risks are fine-grained visual details that captions fail to capture. A diagnostic multiple-choice probe supplying the safety knowledge directly shows models score over 80% (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B 91.0%, LLaVA-OneVision 97.0%, InternVL2.5-8B-MPO 96.5%) versus 27-36% on the corresponding open-ended unsafe subset, indicating the bottleneck is applying known safety knowledge via cross-modal reasoning, not lack of knowledge itself. Reasoning-tuned models (MLRMs) show inconsistent effects relative to their base models: R1-OneVision is 6.36 points worse than base Qwen2.5-VL-7B, while Mixed-R1 (+2.78) and MM-Eureka (+1.69, vs. InternVL2.5-8B base) improve.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SaLAD, a 2,013-sample multimodal (image-text) benchmark that measures whether MLLMs recognize implicit, non-adversarial safety hazards embedded in everyday assistant queries (e.g., 'my sister is 1.2m tall, should she ride this amusement ride?') and respond with an explicit, informative safety warning rather than a generic refusal or an unsafe/oversensitive answer. It is built via a 3-step pipeline (mine 100K real user queries -> cluster into a 10-category taxonomy via K-Means/Sentence-BERT with K chosen at 10 by Calinski-Harabasz index -> pair with authentic (non-AI-generated) real-world images such that the image cannot be inferred purely from text -> write explicit safety-warning references), includes a balanced safe/unsafe split to jointly test unsafe-detection and oversensitivity, and proposes an LLM-as-judge 'safety-warning-based' evaluation metric (Accuracy Rate) validated against human annotation (GPT-4o: Acc 93.85/F1 94.83 on a human-labeled subset, chosen over GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini). The authors evaluate 18 MLLMs (15 MLLMs + 3 multimodal reasoning models) plus three existing safety-alignment methods (VLGuard, MIS, SPA-VL) and a safety-prompting baseline on SaLAD.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial/attack paper: the paper explicitly distinguishes itself from jailbreak-style benchmarks (e.g., FigStep, MM-SafetyBench, HADES) that use adversarially crafted prompts to elicit unsafe MLLM outputs. SaLAD's scenario is a benign user asking a routine daily-life question (health, food, travel, home, sports, study, activity, research, game, office) where risk is incidentally present in the image and the model must proactively warn about it; there is no attacker, no jailbreak prompt, and no adversarial perturbation. The paper frames this as testing 'implicit' safety/helpfulness failure rather than a security vulnerability, so no threat model (attacker capability, adversary goal) is defined or applicable.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: dataset size (2,013 samples) is relatively small given the difficulty/cost of sourcing authentic real-world image-text pairs meeting the 'no visual safety information leakage' constraint (though they argue it is sufficient to expose critical vulnerabilities). Evaluation relies on LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) rather than full human annotation for cost/scalability reasons; they report GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini track human judgment most closely (evaluation cost ~$3 for GPT-4o, ~$0.2 for GPT-4o-mini) but this is still a proxy metric, not ground truth. The paper does not claim broader generalization beyond the 10 curated daily-life categories, and results for safety-alignment retrofits were only tested on 3 open-source backbones (not the full 18-model pool).

datasets_used: SaLAD (2,013 real-world image-text samples, 10 categories, own construction)
100K raw MTurk user queries (candidate pool, pre-filtering, own collection)
400-sample category-balanced subset of SaLAD used for ablation/ diagnosis experiments
quantitative_results: Best-model unsafe-subset Accuracy Rate: Claude 3.7 Sonnet 57.20% (overall 77.05%, safe 99.58%)
Average unsafe-subset Accuracy Rate across all 18 models: 30.65%
Weakest open-source model Deepseek-VL2-Tiny: unsafe 10.93%, safe 89.08%, overall 47.54%
Judge selection: GPT-4o Acc 93.85 / F1 94.83 vs GPT-4o-mini Acc 92.75/F1 93.95, GPT-5 Acc 83.25/F1 87.21, GPT-5-mini Acc 83.50/F1 87.10 (vs. human labels)
VLGuard on Llama-3.2-Vision-11B: overall drops from 63.00% (vanilla) to 42.00%; safe drops 94.00%->54.50%; unsafe changes 32.00%->29.50%
SPA-VL on Qwen2.5-VL-7B: unsafe accuracy rises ~1%, from 33.00% to 35.00% (overall 66.50%->67.50%)
Image captions vs. images: Llama-3.2-Vision-11B gains only ~4.75% with captions; no gain for Qwen2.5-VL-7B or InternVL2.5-8B-MPO

Diagnostic multiple-choice (knowledge-given) accuracy vs. open-ended unsafe-subset accuracy: Qwen2.5-VL-7B 91.0% vs 33.0%; Llama-3.2-Vision-11B 87.5% vs 32.0%; InternVL2.5-8B-MPO 96.5% vs 28.5%; LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B 81.5% vs 27.0%; LLaVA-OneVision 97.0% vs 36.0%
MLRM vs. base-model deltas: R1-OneVision -6.36% vs Qwen2.5-VL-7B; Mixed-R1 +2.78%; MM-Eureka +1.69% vs InternVL2.5-8B
baselines_compared: 18 evaluated MLLMs/MLRMs: GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Claude 3.7 Sonnet (closed-source); LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B, LLaVA-OneVision, Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, InternVL2.5-8B, InternVL2.5-8B-MPO, MiniCPM-V 2.6, Deepseek-VL2-Tiny, Idefics3-8B-Llama3, mPLUG-Owl3-7B, Llama-3.2-Vision-11B (open-source); R1-OneVision, Mixed-R1, MM-Eureka (multimodal reasoning models)
Safety-alignment/defense methods retrofitted onto 3 backbones: VLGuard, MIS, SPA-VL, plus a generic 'Safety Prompt' baseline

Comparison benchmarks discussed for scope/coverage (Table 1, not re-run experimentally in the main results): FigStep, MM-SafetyBench, HADES, VLSafe, VLSBench, MOSSBench, MMSafeAware, JailFlipBench, MSSBench, PaSBench, SIUO
